era_context: The late 14th century in Europe is marked by the fragmentation of feudal authority, the Hundred Years' War between England and France, and the early Italian Renaissance. In Asia, the Ming Dynasty has recently consolidated power in China (1368), while the Ottoman Empire expands in Anatolia and the Balkans. The Avignon Papacy has ended (1377), but the Western Schism looms. This is an era of plague aftermath, religious reform movements, and the emergence of early nation-states.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; oral traditions not systematically recorded)
Southeast Asia (sparse European documentation; local chronicles less accessible to Western scholarship)
Mesoamerica and South America (Aztec and Inca records exist but are fragmentary or post-conquest)

events.title: Battle of Castile: Pedro I defeated by Henry of Trastámara
events.description: Henry of Trastámara, with support from French mercenaries and Aragonese forces, defeats his half-brother Pedro I (Pedro the Cruel) at the Battle of Nájera in March 1366. Pedro flees to Portugal and then to Aquitaine, seeking English support. This marks a major shift in Castilian succession and draws the kingdom into the broader Hundred Years' War dynamics. The conflict reflects both internal Castilian instability and the intervention of major European powers.
key_figures: Henry of Trastámara (claimant to Castilian throne)
Bertrand du Guesclin (French military commander supporting Henry)

sources.name: Pedro López de Ayala, Crónica del Rey Don Pedro

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in multiple near-contemporary chronicles. Dating to March 1366 is secure.

events.title: Tamerlane (Timur) consolidates power in Transoxiana
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), a Turco-Mongol military leader, continues his consolidation of power in Transoxiana after his rise in the 1360s. By 1366, he has eliminated rival khans and established himself as the dominant military force in the region. This marks the beginning of his transformation from regional warlord to empire-builder, setting the stage for his vast conquests across Asia and the Middle East in the following decades.

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafarnama (Book of Victories)
Ibn Arabshah, Aja'ib al-Maqdur fi Nawa'ib Timur

events.certainty_note: Timur's rise is documented in later Persian sources. The exact chronology of his early consolidation (1360–1370) is somewhat uncertain; sources vary on precise dates. 1366 is approximate for his dominance in Transoxiana.

events.title: Papal conclave elects Urban VI; beginning of Western Schism tensions
events.description: Urban VI (Bartolomeo Prignano) is elected Pope in April 1366, following the death of Urban V. Urban VI's abrasive personality and reform agenda alienate the French cardinals, setting the stage for the Western Schism (which formally begins in 1378 with the election of Clement VII). This election marks a critical moment in the Church's institutional crisis, as the papacy's return to Rome from Avignon creates tensions between Italian and French ecclesiastical factions.
events.certainty_note: Urban VI's election is well-documented in papal records and contemporary correspondence. The date (April 1366) is secure.

events.title: Zheng He's predecessor Yongle Emperor consolidates Ming Dynasty control
events.description: The Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di) continues consolidating Ming Dynasty power after the dynasty's establishment in 1368. By 1366, the Ming have secured control over most of China proper and are beginning to expand their military reach. This period sees the construction of major public works, including the Forbidden City (begun 1406), and the establishment of the tributary system that will define Ming foreign relations. The stability achieved by 1366 enables the later maritime expeditions of Zheng He.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di, r. 1402–1424, but consolidating power in 1366 under his father)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Zhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu Emperor) edicts and records

events.certainty_note: Ming Dynasty consolidation is well-documented in official records. The year 1366 falls within the Hongwu Emperor's reign (1368–1398), during which the dynasty's administrative and military foundations were laid.

events.title: Ottoman Empire expands in the Balkans; capture of Adrianople approaches
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Murad I, continues its expansion into the Balkans. By 1366, Ottoman forces have secured much of Thrace and are pressing toward Adrianople (Edirne), which will fall in 1369. This expansion marks the beginning of Ottoman dominance in southeastern Europe and the decline of Byzantine power. The capture of Adrianople will make it the Ottoman capital and a base for further Balkan conquests.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the 1360s is documented in later Ottoman and Byzantine sources. The exact chronology of individual campaigns is sometimes uncertain; 1366 is approximate for the state of Ottoman expansion at this time.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Battle of Nájera (1366-001) is sometimes overstated in Western historiography as a decisive turning point; modern scholarship notes that Pedro I's position remained contested and Henry's rule was unstable until 1369. The characterization of Timur's consolidation (1366-002) as a singular event is problematic—his rise was gradual and contested, and sources disagree on the precise chronology. Urban VI's election (1366-003) is sometimes presented as the direct cause of the Western Schism, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the schism resulted from deeper structural tensions in the Church, not solely from Urban VI's personality. The Ottoman expansion narrative (1366-005) can be read as teleological—contemporary sources do not always present Ottoman growth as inevitable or as the primary concern of regional powers at this moment.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1366 is moderate but heavily weighted toward Europe and the Mediterranean. Western chronicles (Froissart, López de Ayala) provide rich detail on Castilian and English affairs but are often biased toward court perspectives. Ottoman and Byzantine sources are later compilations, introducing chronological uncertainty. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are detailed but reflect official historiography. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are largely absent from surviving written records for this year. The historical record privileges political and military events over economic, social, or cultural developments.

graph_edges.note: Henry of Trastámara's victory in 1366 leads to his final consolidation of power in 1369
Timur's consolidation in 1366 is part of his broader rise to empire-builder status
Urban VI's election and policies contribute to the tensions that erupt in the Western Schism of 1378
Ottoman expansion in 1366 culminates in the fall of Adrianople in 1369
